contents.value: **if** — Shell Script keyword.
**then** — Shell Script keyword.
**else** — Shell Script keyword.
**elif** — Shell Script keyword.
**fi** — Shell Script keyword.
**echo** — Built-in function/command in Shell Script.
**printf** — Built-in function/command in Shell Script.
**read** — Built-in function/command in Shell Script.
**test** — Built-in function/command in Shell Script.
**cd** — Built-in function/command in Shell Script.

```bash
pwd [-LP]
```
Print the absolute pathname of the current working directory. `-L` displays the logical path preserving symlinks (default). `-P` displays the physical path with all symlinks resolved.

```bash
export [-fn] [-p] [name[=value] ...]
```
Set environment variables for child processes. Variables marked for export are inherited by any subsequently executed commands. `-n` removes the export property. `-f` exports shell functions. `-p` lists all exported variables.

```bash
source filename [arguments]
# or: . filename [arguments]
```
Read and execute commands from a file in the **current** shell environment. Unlike executing a script (which runs in a subshell), sourced files can modify variables, functions, and aliases in the calling shell.

```bash
alias [-p] [name[=value] ...]
```
Define or display command aliases. An alias substitutes a string for the first word of a simple command. Aliases are expanded when a command is read, not when it is executed. Use `\command` to bypass an alias.

```bash
trap [-lp] [action] [signal ...]
```
Execute a command when the shell receives a signal or on certain events. Common signals: `EXIT` (on shell exit), `ERR` (on command failure with `set -e`), `INT` (Ctrl+C), `TERM` (termination), `DEBUG` (before every command), `RETURN` (after function/source return). Use `trap - signal` to reset to default.

```bash
set [-abefhkmnptuvxBCEHPT] [-o option-name] [--] [arg ...]
```
Set or unset shell options and positional parameters. Common options:
- `-e` — exit immediately if a command exits with non-zero status
- `-u` — treat unset variables as an error
- `-x` — print commands and arguments as they are executed
- `-o pipefail` — return the exit status of the rightmost failed command in a pipeline
- `--` — end of options; remaining args become positional parameters

```bash
declare [-aAfFgiIlnrtux] [-p] [name[=value] ...]
```
Declare variables and set their attributes. Flags: `-i` (integer), `-a` (indexed array), `-A` (associative array), `-r` (readonly), `-x` (export), `-l` (lowercase on assignment), `-u` (uppercase on assignment), `-n` (nameref), `-g` (global scope in functions). Inside functions, `declare` creates local variables unless `-g` is specified.

```bash
local [-aAfFgiIlnrtux] [name[=value] ...]
```
Declare a variable as local to a function. The variable is visible only within the function and its child functions. Accepts the same attribute flags as `declare`. Can only be used within a function body.

```bash
# List form:
for name [in word ...]; do
    command-list
done

# C-style form:
for (( expr1; expr2; expr3 )); do
    command-list
done
```
Iterate over a list of words or use arithmetic expressions. In the list form, if `in word ...` is omitted, iterates over `"$@"`. The variable `name` is set to each word in turn.

```bash
while command-list; do
    command-list
done
```
Execute commands repeatedly as long as the test command-list returns exit status 0. Commonly used with `read` to process files line by line: `while IFS= read -r line; do ... done < file`.

```bash
until command-list; do
    command-list
done
```
Execute commands repeatedly until the test command-list returns exit status 0. Logically equivalent to `while ! command-list; do ... done`.

```bash
case word in
    pattern1 | pattern2) commands ;;
    pattern3) commands ;;&
    pattern4) commands ;&
    *) default-commands ;;
esac
```
Pattern matching construct. Each pattern is a glob pattern. `;;` terminates the clause. `;&` falls through to the next clause unconditionally. `;;&` tests the next pattern. `*` matches anything (default case). Patterns support `*`, `?`, `[...]`, and extended globs if `extglob` is set.

```bash
select name [in word ...]; do
    command-list
done
```
Create a numbered menu from `word` list. Displays items with numbers, prompts with `$PS3`, stores the chosen word in `name` and the user's raw input in `$REPLY`. Loops until `break` or EOF (Ctrl+D). If `in word ...` is omitted, uses positional parameters.

```bash
shift [n]
```
Shift positional parameters to the left by `n` positions (default 1). After `shift`, `$1` takes the value formerly in `$2`, `$2` takes `$3`, and so on. `$#` is decremented. Commonly used in argument-parsing loops.

```bash
eval [arg ...]
```
Concatenate all arguments into a single command string and execute it in the current shell. Useful for executing dynamically constructed commands. **Use with caution** — eval can introduce code injection vulnerabilities if used with untrusted input.

```bash
exec [-cl] [-a name] [command [arguments ...]] [redirections ...]
```
Replace the current shell process with the specified command (no new process is created). If no command is given, redirections apply to the current shell. `-c` causes the command to execute with an empty environment. Commonly used for file descriptor manipulation: `exec 3>file`, `exec > >(tee log)`.

```bash
return [n]
```
Return from a shell function or sourced script with exit status `n`. If `n` is omitted, returns the exit status of the last command executed. Only valid inside a function or a sourced (`.`/`source`) script.

```bash
exit [n]
```
Exit the shell with status `n`. If `n` is omitted, the status is that of the last command executed. Convention: 0 = success, 1 = general error, 2 = misuse of built-in, 126 = not executable, 127 = command not found, 128+N = killed by signal N.

```bash
readonly [-aAfp] [name[=value] ...]
```
Mark variables or functions as read-only. Readonly variables cannot be reassigned or unset. `-a` for indexed arrays, `-A` for associative arrays, `-f` for functions. `-p` displays all readonly names.

```bash
getopts optstring name [args]
```
Parse command-line options. Each call processes one option. `optstring` lists valid option letters; append `:` for options requiring arguments. On success, stores the option in `name`, the argument in `$OPTARG`, and advances `$OPTIND`. Returns non-zero when all options are processed.

```bash
wait [-fn] [-p varname] [id ...]
```
Wait for background processes to finish. Without arguments, waits for all child processes. With a PID/job ID, waits for that specific process. `-n` waits for the next job to terminate and returns its exit status. `-p varname` stores the PID of the waited process (Bash 5.1+).

```bash
shopt [-pqsu] [-o] [optname ...]
```
Set or unset shell optional behavior. `-s` enables, `-u` disables. Common options:
- `extglob` — extended pattern matching (`?(pat)`, `*(pat)`, `+(pat)`, `@(pat)`, `!(pat)`)
- `globstar` — `**` matches directories recursively
- `nullglob` — unmatched globs expand to nothing
- `dotglob` — globs match dotfiles
- `nocasematch` — case-insensitive matching in `case` and `[[`

```bash
$?
```
The exit status of the most recently executed foreground pipeline. `0` indicates success, non-zero indicates failure. After a pipeline, `$?` reflects the status of the last command (or rightmost failing command with `set -o pipefail`). Use `${PIPESTATUS[@]}` to access individual exit codes from each pipeline stage.

```bash
$#
```
The number of positional parameters (command-line arguments). In a script, counts the arguments passed on invocation. Inside a function, counts the function's arguments. Does not include `$0`.

```bash
$@
"$@"
```
All positional parameters. When quoted (`"$@"`), each parameter is preserved as a separate word, correctly handling arguments with spaces. This is almost always the correct way to forward arguments. Equivalent to `"$1" "$2" ...`.

```bash
$*
"$*"
```
All positional parameters. When quoted (`"$*"`), expands to a single string with parameters joined by the first character of `$IFS` (default: space). Unquoted, behaves like `$@`. Rarely preferred over `"$@"`.

```bash
$$
```
The process ID (PID) of the current shell. In a subshell `(...)`, `$$` still refers to the parent shell's PID. Use `$BASHPID` to get the actual PID of the current subshell (Bash 4.0+). Commonly used to create unique temporary filenames.

```bash
$!
```
The PID of the most recently backgrounded process (started with `&`). Useful for tracking background jobs: `command & pid=$!; wait $pid`.

```bash
$0
```
The name of the shell or the shell script being executed. In an interactive shell, typically `bash` or `-bash`. In a script, it is the path used to invoke the script. Use `${BASH_SOURCE[0]}` for the actual source file (works inside sourced scripts).

```bash
IFS=$' \t\n'  # default value
```
Internal Field Separator. Determines how the shell splits words after expansion and how `read` splits input. Default: space, tab, newline. Set `IFS=''` to disable splitting. Set `IFS=:` to split PATH-like variables. Often set locally in a subshell or function: `(IFS=,; echo "${array[*]}")`.

```bash
PATH=/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin
```
Colon-separated list of directories the shell searches (left to right) when locating commands. Prepend directories for priority: `PATH="/my/bin:$PATH"`. Append for fallback: `PATH="$PATH:/my/bin"`. Empty components (`::`or leading/trailing `:`) refer to the current directory.

```bash
HOME=/home/username
```
The home directory of the current user. Set at login from `/etc/passwd`. Used as the default directory for `cd` (no arguments), and for tilde expansion (`~` expands to `$HOME`, `~user` to that user's home).

```bash
BASH_SOURCE  # array variable
```
An array whose members are the source filenames for each function in the call stack. `${BASH_SOURCE[0]}` is the file containing the currently executing code. Particularly useful for finding the script's own directory:
```bash
SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
```

```bash
PIPESTATUS  # array variable
```
An array containing the exit statuses of the processes in the most recently executed foreground pipeline. `${PIPESTATUS[0]}` is the first command's status, `${PIPESTATUS[1]}` the second, etc. Useful for checking individual stages: `cmd1 | cmd2; echo "${PIPESTATUS[@]}"`.

```bash
RANDOM
```
Each reference generates a random integer between 0 and 32767. Assigning a value seeds the generator for reproducible sequences. For a range 0 to N-1: `$((RANDOM % N))`. For better randomness in scripts, consider `/dev/urandom`.

```bash
SECONDS
```
The number of seconds since the shell was started. Assigning a value resets the counter:
```bash
SECONDS=0
# ... operations ...
echo "Elapsed: ${SECONDS}s"
```

```bash
FUNCNAME  # array variable
```
An array of function names in the call stack. `${FUNCNAME[0]}` is the current function, `${FUNCNAME[1]}` is the caller, etc. The last element is `main` (for top-level code). Only meaningful inside functions.

```bash
BASH_REMATCH  # array variable
```
Populated by the `=~` operator in `[[ ]]`. Element 0 contains the entire match, elements 1+ contain capture group matches:
```bash
if [[ "hello123" =~ ^([a-z]+)([0-9]+)$ ]]; then
    echo "${BASH_REMATCH[0]}"  # hello123
    echo "${BASH_REMATCH[1]}"  # hello
    echo "${BASH_REMATCH[2]}"  # 123
fi
```

```bash
mapfile [-d delim] [-n count] [-O origin] [-s count] [-t] [-u fd] [-C callback] [-c quantum] [array]
```
Read lines from stdin into an indexed array. Default array is `MAPFILE`. `-t` strips trailing delimiters. `-n count` reads at most N lines. `-s count` skips the first N lines. Also available as `readarray`.
```bash
mapfile -t lines < file.txt
echo "${lines[0]}"
```

```bash
let expression [expression ...]
```
Evaluate arithmetic expressions. Each expression is an arithmetic expression using C-style operators: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%`, `**`, `++`, `--`, comparisons, bitwise and logical operators. Returns 0 if the last expression is non-zero, 1 otherwise. Equivalent to `(( expression ))`.

```bash
history [-c] [-d offset] [n]
history -anrw [filename]
```
Display or manipulate command history. `history n` shows the last N entries. `-c` clears the history list. `-d N` deletes entry N. `-a` appends new entries to the history file. `-r` reads the history file. `-w` writes the current history to the file. `HISTSIZE` controls the in-memory list size, `HISTFILESIZE` the file size.

```bash
unalias [-a] name [name ...]
```
Remove alias definitions. Each `name` is removed from the list of aliases. The `-a` flag removes **all** aliases. Returns success unless a given name is not a defined alias.

```bash
type [-afptP] name [name ...]
```
Indicate how each `name` would be interpreted as a command. `-t` outputs a single word: `alias`, `keyword`, `function`, `builtin`, or `file`. `-a` displays all locations containing an executable named `name`. `-p` returns the disk file path (only for external commands). `-P` forces a PATH search even for builtins.

```bash
unset [-fv] [name ...]
```
Remove variable or function definitions. `-v` removes variables (default), `-f` removes functions. Readonly variables cannot be unset. Returns `0` unless a name is readonly. Unsetting `RANDOM`, `SECONDS`, etc. removes their special behavior.

```bash
[[ expression ]]
```
Extended conditional command. Unlike `[ ]`, supports `&&` and `||` operators, pattern matching with `==` (glob) and `=~` (regex), and no word splitting or pathname expansion inside. Returns `0` (true) or `1` (false). Use `BASH_REMATCH` array to access regex capture groups from `=~`.

```bash
jobs [-lnprs] [jobspec ...]
```
List active jobs. `-l` adds process IDs, `-p` lists PIDs only, `-r` restricts to running jobs, `-s` restricts to stopped jobs, `-n` lists only jobs whose status has changed since last notification. Job specs use `%n` (job number), `%string` (command prefix), `%%` or `%+` (current job), `%-` (previous job).

```bash
kill [-s sigspec] [-n signum] [-sigspec] pid|jobspec ...
kill -l|-L [sigspec]
```
Send signals to processes or jobs. Default signal is `TERM` (15). Common signals: `HUP` (1) — reload config, `INT` (2) — interrupt (Ctrl+C), `KILL` (9) — force kill (uncatchable), `STOP` (19) — pause, `CONT` (18) — resume. `-l` lists all signal names. Use `%jobspec` for background jobs.

```bash
bg [jobspec ...]
```
Resume a suspended job in the background, as if it had been started with `&`. If no `jobspec` is given, the current job (`%%`) is used. The shell prints `[jobnumber] PID` when the job is resumed.

```bash
fg [jobspec]
```
Move a background or stopped job to the foreground, making it the current job. The shell waits for it to complete. If no `jobspec` is given, the current job (`%%`) is used. The resumed command's exit status becomes `$?`.

```bash
pushd [-n] [+n|-n|dir]
```
Push a directory onto the directory stack and `cd` to it. `-n` suppresses the directory change (only modifies the stack). `+n` rotates the stack so the nth directory (counting from the left) is at the top. `dirs` shows the stack. Used with `popd` for navigating multiple directories.

```bash
popd [-n] [+n|-n]
```
Remove the top entry from the directory stack and `cd` to the new top directory. `-n` suppresses the directory change (only modifies the stack). `+n`/`-n` removes the nth entry from the left/right of the stack. Use `dirs` to view the current stack.

```bash
complete [-abcdefgjksuv] [-o option] [-A action] [-G globpat] [-W wordlist] [-F function] [-C command] [-X filterpat] [-P prefix] [-S suffix] name [name ...]
```
Define programmable completion for command `name`. `-F function` calls a shell function to generate completions. `-W wordlist` provides a list of words. `-o filenames` enables filename-style processing. `-o nospace` suppresses trailing space. Use with `compgen` inside completion functions.

```bash
compgen [-abcdefgjksuv] [-o option] [-A action] [-G globpat] [-W wordlist] [-F function] [-C command] [-X filterpat] [-P prefix] [-S suffix] [word]
```
Generate possible completions for `word`. Used within programmable completion functions (invoked by `complete -F`). `-W wordlist` generates from a word list. `-A action` generates from built-in categories (`file`, `directory`, `command`, `variable`, etc.). Output is one match per line.

```bash
enable [-a] [-dnps] [-f filename] [name ...]
```
Enable or disable shell builtin commands. `-n` disables the named builtins, causing the shell to use external commands instead. `-a` lists all builtins showing enabled/disabled state. `-f filename` loads a new builtin from a shared object file. Disabling builtins is useful for testing or replacing them with custom implementations.

```bash
ulimit [-HSabcdefiklmnpqrstuvxPRT] [limit]
```
Get or set user-level resource limits. `-S` for soft limits, `-H` for hard limits. Common options: `-n` (max open files, default 1024), `-u` (max user processes), `-v` (virtual memory in KB), `-s` (stack size in KB), `-c` (core file size), `-f` (max file size). Use `unlimited` to remove a limit. Changes affect the current shell and its children.

```bash
umask [-p] [-S] [mode]
```
Set the file creation permission mask. The mask is bits to **remove** from default permissions (777 for directories, 666 for files). Common values: `022` (owner full, group/others read+execute), `077` (owner only), `002` (group writable). `-S` displays in symbolic form (`u=rwx,g=rx,o=rx`). `-p` outputs in a form reusable as input.

```bash
$_
```
The last argument of the previous command. At shell startup, set to the absolute pathname of the shell or script being executed. After a command runs, set to the last argument of that command. In interactive shells, pressing `Esc .` or `Alt+.` inserts the equivalent value.

```bash
$-
```
The current set of active shell option flags. Each letter represents an enabled option (e.g., `h` for hashall, `i` for interactive, `m` for monitor, `B` for brace expansion, `H` for history expansion). Equivalent to the flags that `set` is currently using. Example: `himBH` for a typical interactive shell.

```bash
PWD
```
The absolute pathname of the current working directory. Automatically updated by `cd`. May contain symlinks unless `cd -P` was used. Equivalent to the output of `pwd -L` (logical path). Set by the shell at startup and after each directory change.

```bash
OLDPWD
```
The previous working directory, set by `cd`. `cd -` changes to `$OLDPWD` and prints the new directory. Unset if `cd` has not been called since the shell started. Useful for toggling between two directories.

```bash
SHELL=/bin/bash
```
The full pathname of the user's login shell, as defined in `/etc/passwd`. This variable is **not** changed when a different shell is invoked — it always reflects the login shell. Programs like terminal emulators use it to determine which shell to start.

```bash
USER
```
The name of the current user. Set at login. Unlike `$EUID` (numeric), `$USER` is the textual username. Equivalent to `$(whoami)` in most cases. Note: this can be overwritten since it's not readonly — use `id -un` for a reliable alternative.

```bash
HOSTNAME
```
The name of the current host, automatically set at shell startup. Equivalent to the output of the `hostname` command. Used in the default prompt (`$PS1`) with `\h` (short hostname) or `\H` (FQDN).

```bash
LINENO
```
The current line number in the script or function being executed (1-based). Dynamically updated as each line is processed. Useful for debugging and error messages:
```bash
echo "Error at line $LINENO" >&2
```
Inside a function, `LINENO` reflects the line within the function, not the script.

```bash
BASH_LINENO  # array variable
```
An array of line numbers corresponding to the call stack. `${BASH_LINENO[0]}` is the line number in the calling script where the current function was invoked. Used with `FUNCNAME` and `BASH_SOURCE` to construct stack traces:
```bash
echo "Called from ${BASH_SOURCE[1]}:${BASH_LINENO[0]}"
```

```bash
OPTARG
```
Set by the `getopts` builtin. Contains the argument value when processing an option that requires one (indicated by `:` after the option letter in the optstring). When an invalid option is encountered, `OPTARG` holds the invalid option character if optstring starts with `:`.

```bash
OPTIND
```
The index of the next argument to be processed by `getopts`. Initialized to `1` each time the shell starts or a script is invoked. After `getopts` finishes, `shift $((OPTIND - 1))` removes all processed options, leaving only non-option arguments in `$@`.

```bash
REPLY
```
Default variable for `read` when no variable name is supplied: `read` stores the entire input line in `$REPLY`. Also set by the `select` construct to the user's raw input string (the number they typed). Always a string value.

```bash
BASH_VERSION
```
A string describing the version of this Bash instance (e.g., `5.2.15(1)-release`). For structured version info, use `BASH_VERSINFO` array: `[0]`=major, `[1]`=minor, `[2]`=patch, `[3]`=build, `[4]`=status, `[5]`=machine type. Useful for feature detection:
```bash
if ((BASH_VERSINFO[0] >= 4)); then echo "Bash 4+"; fi
```

```bash
EUID
```
The effective user ID of the current user (read-only). `0` indicates root/superuser. May differ from `$UID` when running with `sudo` or setuid programs. Common use:
```bash
if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then echo "Run as root"; exit 1; fi
```

```bash
UID
```
The real user ID of the current user (read-only). Unlike `$EUID`, `$UID` reflects the original user even under `sudo`. Both values are integers. Use `id -u` for a portable equivalent.

```bash
name=(value1 value2 ...)
name[index]=value
```
Indexed arrays hold ordered lists of values, accessed by integer index starting at `0`. Key operations: `${arr[@]}` (all elements), `${#arr[@]}` (length), `${!arr[@]}` (all indices), `arr+=(val)` (append), `${arr[@]:offset:length}` (slice), `unset 'arr[i]'` (remove element). Arrays can be sparse — indices don't need to be contiguous.

```bash
declare -A name=([key1]=value1 [key2]=value2)
```
Associative arrays use arbitrary strings as keys. **Must** be declared with `declare -A` before use. Key operations: `${map[key]}` (get), `map[key]=value` (set), `${!map[@]}` (all keys), `${#map[@]}` (size), `unset 'map[key]'` (delete). Available since Bash 4.0. Cannot be exported to child processes.

```bash
${parameter...}
```
Bash parameter expansion provides extensive string manipulation:
- `${#var}` — length
- `${var:offset:length}` — substring
- `${var:-default}` — default value
- `${var:=default}` — assign default
- `${var:+alternate}` — alternate value
- `${var:?error}` — error if unset
- `${var#pattern}` / `${var##pattern}` — remove prefix (shortest/longest)
- `${var%pattern}` / `${var%%pattern}` — remove suffix
- `${var/pat/rep}` / `${var//pat/rep}` — replace first/all
- `${var^}` / `${var^^}` — uppercase first/all
- `${var,}` / `${var,,}` — lowercase first/all

```bash
$(( expression ))
(( expression ))
let expression
```
Bash integer arithmetic supports C-style operators: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%`, `**` (power), `++`, `--`, bitwise (`&`, `|`, `^`, `~`, `<<`, `>>`), logical (`&&`, `||`, `!`), ternary (`?:`), comma, and all compound assignments (`+=`, `-=`, etc.). Variables are referenced by name without `$`. Use `$(( ))` for substitution, `(( ))` for conditions/side-effects.

```zsh
# Most useful options:
setopt AUTO_CD              # Type dir name to cd
setopt AUTO_PUSHD           # cd pushes to dir stack
setopt GLOB_DOTS            # Include dotfiles in globs
setopt EXTENDED_GLOB        # ^, ~, # glob operators
setopt NO_CASE_GLOB         # Case-insensitive matching
setopt CORRECT              # Suggest corrections
setopt INTERACTIVE_COMMENTS # Allow # comments
setopt PROMPT_SUBST         # Expand variables in prompt

# History options:
setopt SHARE_HISTORY        # Sync across sessions
setopt HIST_IGNORE_ALL_DUPS # Remove older duplicates
setopt HIST_IGNORE_SPACE    # Skip space-prefixed cmds
setopt HIST_VERIFY          # Show before executing !!

# Disable an option:
unsetopt BEEP
setopt NO_BEEP  # equivalent
```
**setopt** enables shell options. Options can be written NO_BEEP or NOBEEP or nobeep. Use `setopt` alone to list all enabled options.

```zsh
# Autoload marks functions for lazy loading from fpath:
autoload -Uz compinit && compinit  # Completion system
autoload -Uz vcs_info              # VCS info for prompt
autoload -Uz add-zsh-hook          # Hook management
autoload -Uz colors && colors      # Color variables
autoload -Uz zmv                   # Advanced rename
autoload -Uz zargs                 # xargs equivalent
autoload -Uz zcalc                 # Calculator

# Create your own autoloaded function:
# 1. Create file ~/.zsh/functions/myfunc
# 2. Write function body (no function wrapper needed)
# 3. Add to fpath:
fpath=(~/.zsh/functions $fpath)
autoload -Uz myfunc
```
**autoload -Uz** is the standard way to load functions. `-U` suppresses alias expansion, `-z` forces zsh-style loading. Functions are loaded from `$fpath` on first call.

```zsh
# Prompt escape sequences:
# %n  username       %m  hostname
# %~  current dir    %d  full path
# %#  # if root      %?  exit status
# %D  date           %*  time HH:MM:SS
# %j  number of jobs

# Colors:
# %F{red}text%f      foreground color
# %K{blue}text%k     background color
# %B bold %b         %U underline %u

# Examples:
PROMPT='%F{cyan}%n%f@%F{green}%m%f:%F{yellow}%~%f %# '
RPROMPT='%F{gray}%*%f'

# Git info in prompt:
autoload -Uz vcs_info
precmd() { vcs_info }
zstyle ':vcs_info:git:*' formats '(%b)'
setopt PROMPT_SUBST
PROMPT='%~ ${vcs_info_msg_0_} %# '
```
**PROMPT / PS1** sets the primary prompt. Use `%F{color}...%f` for colors. Enable `PROMPT_SUBST` for variable expansion in prompts.

```zsh
# List all bindings:
bindkey

# Emacs mode (default):
bindkey -e

# Vi mode:
bindkey -v

# Common bindings:
bindkey '^R'    history-incremental-search-backward
bindkey '^S'    history-incremental-search-forward
bindkey '^P'    up-line-or-history
bindkey '^N'    down-line-or-history
bindkey '^A'    beginning-of-line
bindkey '^E'    end-of-line
bindkey '^K'    kill-line
bindkey '^W'    backward-kill-word
bindkey '^[[A'  up-line-or-search        # Up arrow
bindkey '^[[B'  down-line-or-search      # Down arrow
bindkey '^[[3~' delete-char              # Delete key

# Show what a key does:
bindkey '^R'
```
**bindkey** maps keys to ZLE (Zsh Line Editor) widgets. Use `^X` for Ctrl+X, `^[` for Alt/Escape. `bindkey -l` lists keymaps.

```zsh
# Completion styling:
zstyle ':completion:*' menu select              # Tab menu
zstyle ':completion:*' matcher-list \            
    'm:{a-z}={A-Z}' 'r:|[._-]=* r:|=*'         # Case insensitive
zstyle ':completion:*' list-colors ${(s.:.)LS_COLORS}  # Colorize
zstyle ':completion:*' use-cache on              # Cache completions
zstyle ':completion:*' cache-path ~/.zsh/cache
zstyle ':completion:*' group-name ''             # Group by type
zstyle ':completion:*:descriptions' format '%F{yellow}-- %d --%f'
zstyle ':completion:*:warnings' format '%F{red}No matches%f'

# Kill command completion:
zstyle ':completion:*:*:kill:*:processes' list-colors '=(#b) #([0-9]#)*=0=01;31'
zstyle ':completion:*:kill:*' command 'ps -u $USER -o pid,%cpu,tty,cmd'

# SSH host completion:
zstyle ':completion:*:ssh:*' hosts $(awk '/^Host / {print $2}' ~/.ssh/config)
```
**zstyle** configures the completion system. Pattern format: `:completion:function:completer:command:argument:tag`. Incredibly powerful for customizing tab completion behavior.

```zsh
# Basic globs:
*           # Any file
**/*        # Recursive
*.{js,ts}   # Alternation

# Glob qualifiers (after closing paren):
ls *(.)     # Regular files only
ls *(/)     # Directories only
ls *(@)     # Symlinks only
ls *(*)     # Executables
ls *(om)    # Sort by modification time
ls *(oL)    # Sort by size
ls *(Lk+100)  # Files > 100KB
ls *(mh-1)    # Modified in last hour
ls *(mw-1)    # Modified in last week
ls *(.om[1,5]) # 5 most recent files
ls *(N)     # Null glob (no error if empty)

# Extended glob (setopt EXTENDED_GLOB):
ls ^*.bak    # NOT matching *.bak
ls *.txt~secret*  # *.txt except secret*
ls (#i)*.jpg      # Case-insensitive
```
**Zsh globbing** is extremely powerful. Glob qualifiers filter by file type, size, date, permissions. Extended glob adds negation (^), exclusion (~), case-insensitive (#i).

```zsh
# Basic:
${var}           # Value
${#var}          # Length
${var:-default}  # Default if unset
${var:=default}  # Assign default
${var:+alt}      # Alternative if set
${var:?error}    # Error if unset

# Substring:
${var:offset:len}  # Substring
${var#pat}   # Remove shortest prefix
${var##pat}  # Remove longest prefix
${var%pat}   # Remove shortest suffix
${var%%pat}  # Remove longest suffix
${var/pat/rep}   # Replace first
${var//pat/rep}  # Replace all

# Zsh-specific flags:
${(U)var}    # UPPERCASE
${(L)var}    # lowercase
${(C)var}    # Capitalize Words
${(s:.:)var} # Split on '.'
${(j:,:)arr} # Join with ','
${(u)arr}    # Unique elements
${(o)arr}    # Sort
${(f)var}    # Split on newlines
${(F)arr}    # Join with newlines
${(k)hash}   # Keys of assoc array
${(v)hash}   # Values of assoc array
```
**Parameter expansion** is zsh's most powerful feature. The `(flags)` syntax provides string/array transformations without external commands.

```zsh
# Initialize:
autoload -Uz compinit && compinit

# Custom completion function:
_myapp() {
    local -a commands
    commands=(
        'start:Start the application'
        'stop:Stop the application'
        'deploy:Deploy to production'
    )
    
    _arguments \
        '-v[Verbose output]' \
        '-c[Config file]:config file:_files -g "*.yml"' \
        '1:command:->cmd' && return
    
    case $state in
        cmd) _describe 'command' commands ;;
    esac
}
compdef _myapp myapp

# Useful completion functions:
# _files      - complete files
# _directories - complete directories
# _users      - complete usernames
# _hosts      - complete hostnames
# _describe   - describe and complete from array
# _arguments  - full argument specification
```
**Completion system** provides intelligent tab completion. Write `_command` functions using `_arguments` for option specs and `_describe` for subcommands.

```zsh
# Install:
sh -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/master/tools/install.sh)"

# Configuration in ~/.zshrc:
export ZSH="$HOME/.oh-my-zsh"
ZSH_THEME="robbyrussell"  # or: agnoster, powerlevel10k

plugins=(
    git           # Git aliases (gst, gco, gp, gl)
    docker        # Docker completions
    kubectl       # k8s completions & aliases
    z             # Frecency-based cd
    fzf           # Fuzzy finder integration
    colored-man-pages
    command-not-found
    zsh-autosuggestions
    zsh-syntax-highlighting
)

source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh

# Custom plugins: ~/.oh-my-zsh/custom/plugins/
# Custom themes: ~/.oh-my-zsh/custom/themes/

# Update: omz update
# List plugins: ls ~/.oh-my-zsh/plugins/
```
**Oh My Zsh** is a framework for managing zsh configuration. 300+ plugins and 150+ themes. Use sparingly — too many plugins slow startup.

```zsh
autoload -Uz zmv

# Rename with patterns:
zmv '(*).txt' '$1.md'          # .txt -> .md
zmv '(*).JPG' '${(L)1}.jpg'   # UPPERCASE -> lowercase
zmv -W '*.jpeg' '*.jpg'        # Simple wildcard rename

# Complex renames:
zmv '(**/)(*).txt' '$1${2// /_}.txt'  # Replace spaces
zmv -n '(*).log' '$1_$(date +%Y%m%d).log'  # Dry run (-n)

# Copy / Link variants:
zcp '*.txt' backup/             # Copy with patterns
zln -s '*.conf' links/          # Symlink with patterns
```
**zmv** is a powerful batch rename tool. Use `-n` for dry run. `-W` enables simpler wildcard syntax. Supports all zsh parameter expansion.

```zsh
# Hook functions run at specific times:
precmd()  { }  # Before each prompt
preexec() { }  # Before each command ($1=typed, $2=expanded)
chpwd()   { }  # After directory change
periodic(){ }  # Every $PERIOD seconds
zshaddhistory() { }  # Before adding to history

# Multiple functions per hook:
autoload -Uz add-zsh-hook
add-zsh-hook precmd my_precmd_1
add-zsh-hook precmd my_precmd_2
add-zsh-hook -d precmd my_precmd_1  # Remove

# Example: Timer for commands
__timer_preexec() { __timer=$EPOCHREALTIME }
__timer_precmd() {
    [[ -n $__timer ]] || return
    local elapsed=$(( EPOCHREALTIME - __timer ))
    (( elapsed > 3 )) && printf 'Elapsed: %.2fs\n' $elapsed
    unset __timer
}
add-zsh-hook preexec __timer_preexec
add-zsh-hook precmd __timer_precmd
```
**Hook functions** execute at shell events. Use `add-zsh-hook` to register multiple handlers. `preexec` receives the command as arguments.

```shell
builtin name [args]
```
Execute a builtin command, ignoring functions and aliases with the same name.

```shell
compdef function command ...
```
Associate a completion function with commands. Part of the completion system.

```shell
dirs [-clpv]
```
Display directory stack. -v: verbose listing with indices. -c: clear stack.

```shell
emulate [-lLR] mode
```
Set shell emulation mode: zsh, sh, ksh, csh. -L: local to function. -R: reset all options.

```shell
fc [-e editor] [-nlrdDfEim] [first [last]]
```
Fix command — list, edit, or re-execute history entries.

```shell
float [-Hg] [-E n] [-F n] [name[=value] ...]
```
Declare floating-point variables.

```shell
functions [-mMtTuUz] [name ...]
```
Display or set attributes on functions. Same as typeset -f.

```shell
hash [-dfmrv] [name[=value] ...]
```
Manage command hash table. Maps command names to paths for faster lookup.

```shell
integer [-i base] [name[=value] ...]
```
Declare integer variables. Arithmetic is performed on assignments.

```shell
print [-abcDilmnNoOpPrsz] [-u fd] [-f fmt] [args ...]
```
Print arguments. More powerful than echo. -P: prompt expansion. -f: printf format. -l: one per line.

```shell
rehash
```
Rebuild command hash table. Run after installing new programs.

```shell
typeset [-aAfFghilrtuxmHLRZE] [name[=value] ...]
```
Declare and set attributes on variables. The most versatile variable declaration command.

```shell
unfunction name ...
```
Remove function definitions.

```shell
unsetopt [option ...]
```
Disable shell options.

```shell
whence [-acfmpsvw] name ...
```
Show how each name would be interpreted. -p: search path only. -v: verbose.

```shell
where name ...
```
Display all locations of a command.

```shell
which [-aps] name ...
```
Show full path of command.

```shell
zcompile [-cz] [-R|-M] file [name ...]
```
Compile zsh scripts to wordcode for faster loading.

```shell
zle [-aAcCDFgGIKlLmNRU] [widget]
```
Zsh Line Editor — manage widgets. -N: create widget from function. -C: create completion widget.

```shell
zmodload [-diLu] [module ...]
```
Load or unload zsh modules. Common: zsh/datetime, zsh/stat, zsh/regex, zsh/net/tcp.

```shell
zparseopts [-DEKA] specs ...
```
Parse command-line options. -D: remove parsed options. -E: don't stop at first non-option. -A assoc: store in associative array.

```shell
PROMPT=string
```
Primary prompt string. Supports: %n (user), %m (host), %~ (dir), %F{color}...%f (color), %B...%b (bold), %# (# for root, %% for user).

```shell
RPROMPT=string
```
Right-side prompt string. Commonly shows time, git status, or exit code.

```shell
PS1=string
```
Same as PROMPT. Primary prompt.

```shell
PS2=string
```
Continuation prompt (shown for multi-line commands). Default: '%_> '.

```shell
HISTFILE=path
```
Path to the history file. Default: ~/.zsh_history.

```shell
HISTSIZE=number
```
Maximum number of history entries in memory.

```shell
SAVEHIST=number
```
Number of history entries saved to HISTFILE.

```shell
FPATH=dirs
```
Function search path (colon-separated). Directories where autoloaded functions are found.

```shell
fpath=(dirs)
```
Array form of FPATH. Tied to FPATH — changes to one affect the other.

```shell
path=(dirs)
```
Array form of PATH. Tied to PATH.

```shell
CDPATH=dirs
```
Directories searched by cd. cd target tries CDPATH entries as parents.

```shell
KEYTIMEOUT=n
```
Timeout for key sequences (1/100th second). Default: 40 (0.4s). Set to 1 for fast vi-mode switch.

```shell
WORDCHARS=chars
```
Characters considered part of a word for word-based operations. Default includes many special chars.

```shell
ZSH_VERSION
```
Current zsh version string.

```shell
TERM=string
```
Terminal type for terminfo lookups.
